---
title: "Environments and Dependencies"
description: "Use virtual environments, install packages, and understand pyproject.toml."
execute:
  echo: true
---

## Why this matters

Use virtual environments, install packages, and understand pyproject.toml.

This lesson combines related subtopics that belong together in one learning
conversation. You will still pause for a quiz after each section, but you do not
need to jump between separate pages while building one clear explanation.

::: {.questions}
### Questions

By the end of this lesson, you should be able to answer:

- How do the sections in **Environments and Dependencies** fit together?
- Which small example demonstrates each section?
- Which debugging clue should I check first for each section?
:::

::: {.learning-objectives}
### Learning objectives

You will practice how to:

- explain the shared concept for this lesson;
- use each section as one step in a larger workflow;
- complete 3 short section quizzes before moving on;
- connect examples, mistakes, and debugging routines.
:::

## Lesson map

- **1. Virtual Environments** — Create isolated Python environments for project dependencies.
- **2. Installing Packages** — Install third-party libraries and record what your project needs.
- **3. pyproject.toml** — Understand the configuration file used by modern Python projects.

## How environments connect tools and packages

::: {.concept-diagram}
A virtual environment keeps one project's interpreter and installed packages
separate from other projects.

```{mermaid}
%%| echo: false
%%| eval: true
flowchart TD
  project["project folder"] --> venv[".venv<br/>virtual environment"]
  venv --> python["Python interpreter"]
  venv --> packages["installed packages<br/>numpy, pandas, pytest"]
  project --> pyproject["pyproject.toml<br/>dependency list"]
  system["system Python"] -. separate from .-> venv
```

When imports fail, check which Python interpreter is active before reinstalling
packages.
:::

## 1. Virtual Environments

Create isolated Python environments for project dependencies.

::: {.analogy}
### Analogy for this section

A virtual environment is a separate lunchbox: one project's ingredients do not spill
into another's.
:::

### What this means

A virtual environment gives one project its own installed packages.

### First example

Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```bash
python -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate
python -m pip install requests
```

### Step-by-step explanation

1. `python -m venv .venv` — identify the value, name, or action on this line.
2. `source .venv/bin/activate` — identify the value, name, or action on this line.
3. `python -m pip install requests` — identify the value, name, or action on this line.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.challenge}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy the first example into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.debugging-corner}
Installing packages before activating the environment often installs them in the wrong
place. Check which python.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Check your understanding

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "projects-and-environments-virtual-environments",
  "title": "Virtual Environments checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes Virtual Environments?",
      "options": [
        "A virtual environment gives one project its own installed packages.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: A virtual environment gives one project its own installed packages."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: A virtual environment is a separate lunchbox: one project's ingredients do not spill into another's."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after the first example works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## 2. Installing Packages

Install third-party libraries and record what your project needs.

::: {.analogy}
### Analogy for this section

Installing a package is borrowing a specialized tool from a community workshop.
:::

### What this means

Packages extend Python with code written by other people.

### First example

Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```bash
python -m pip install numpy
python -m pip show numpy
```

### Step-by-step explanation

1. `python -m pip install numpy` — identify the value, name, or action on this line.
2. `python -m pip show numpy` — identify the value, name, or action on this line.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.challenge}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy the first example into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.debugging-corner}
Use python -m pip so pip belongs to the Python interpreter you are using.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Check your understanding

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "projects-and-environments-installing-packages",
  "title": "Installing Packages checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes Installing Packages?",
      "options": [
        "Packages extend Python with code written by other people.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: Packages extend Python with code written by other people."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: Installing a package is borrowing a specialized tool from a community workshop."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after the first example works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## 3. pyproject.toml

Understand the configuration file used by modern Python projects.

::: {.analogy}
### Analogy for this section

pyproject.toml is a project recipe card: name, ingredients, tools, and rules in one
place.
:::

### What this means

pyproject.toml records project metadata, dependencies, and tool configuration.

### First example

Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```toml
[project]
name = "my-project"
version = "0.1.0"
dependencies = ["numpy"]
```

### Step-by-step explanation

1. `[project]` — identify the value, name, or action on this line.
2. `name = "my-project"` — identify the value, name, or action on this line.
3. `version = "0.1.0"` — identify the value, name, or action on this line.
4. `dependencies = ["numpy"]` — identify the value, name, or action on this line.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.challenge}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy the first example into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.debugging-corner}
Configuration files are read by tools. A typo may not fail until that tool runs.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Check your understanding

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "projects-and-environments-pyproject-toml",
  "title": "pyproject.toml checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes pyproject.toml?",
      "options": [
        "pyproject.toml records project metadata, dependencies, and tool configuration.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: pyproject.toml records project metadata, dependencies, and tool configuration."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: pyproject.toml is a project recipe card: name, ingredients, tools, and rules in one place."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after the first example works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## Notebook and Colab practice

Open a blank notebook at <https://colab.new>. Use one section at a time: copy the
first example, predict the result, run it, answer the section quiz, and then move
to the next section. This is better than copying the entire page at once.

## Instructor note

<details class="instructor">
<summary>Teaching notes</summary>

- Treat each section as a short teaching episode.
- Pause for the section quiz before introducing the next section.
- Ask learners to compare sections: what stayed the same, and what changed?
- If time is short, teach the first two sections live and assign the rest as practice.

</details>

## Key points

::: {.key-points}
- **Virtual Environments**: A virtual environment gives one project its own installed packages.
- **Installing Packages**: Packages extend Python with code written by other people.
- **pyproject.toml**: pyproject.toml records project metadata, dependencies, and tool configuration.
- Use the section quizzes as gates: review before moving on if a quiz feels uncertain.
:::

## References

- Python modules tutorial: https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/modules.html
- venv documentation: https://docs.python.org/3/library/venv.html
- argparse documentation: https://docs.python.org/3/library/argparse.html
- Python Tutorial: https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/
- Quarto OJS documentation: https://quarto.org/docs/interactive/ojs/
- ipywidgets documentation: https://ipywidgets.readthedocs.io/en/stable/
